one_line_takeaway: Because offline-bandit evaluation reward models (e.g. ImageReward, LAION Aesthetic Predictor) are often shared as public weights, an adversary who can perturb only those weights -- not the logged data or training loop -- can achieve near-100% attack success at imperceptibly small perturbation norms, and the attack gets cheaper as input dimensionality grows, defeating existing reward-corruption-robust bandit defenses that assume a different (active, in-training) attacker.
key_finding: The attack is highly effective and gets EASIER, not harder, as dimensionality grows -- opposite of the usual intuition that high-dimensional problems are 'harder' to attack. On synthetic data all three attack variants (OSA, Trajectory-Free, Full-Trajectory) achieve 100% attack success rate (ASR) on UCB, with OSA dramatically faster (constraint count scaling like O(log T) versus O(T) or O(TK)) while remaining near-optimal (small gap to the QP-optimal solution that shrinks further as d grows). On the two real Hugging Face reward models (ImageReward, LAION Aesthetic Predictor) evaluating five generative models (SD3, SD1.4, Kandinsky 3, Openjourney, SDXL) over 30 GenAI-Bench prompts, roughly 80% of randomly sampled prompts yield an ASR between 90-100%. Targeted optimization is essential: random noise perturbations of the reward weights achieve only about 25% baseline ASR and stay roughly flat even as the noise's l2-norm is increased, i.e. undirected perturbation does not work. For the nonlinear (ReLU network) reward model, attack success requires sufficient hidden-layer width for the NTK linear approximation to hold -- ASR reaches 100% once the layer exceeds 750 neurons. The attack generalizes across bandit algorithms (100% ASR against UCB, ETC, and epsilon-greedy) and defeats existing corruption-robust bandit algorithms designed for a different (active, in-training) threat model: against Fast-Slow (Lykouris et al. 2018, corruption parameter 0.5) the attack achieves 100% ASR with perturbation norm ~0.3 (K in {3,5}, T=1000, d=1000); against epsilon-contamination UCB (Niss & Tewari 2020, epsilon=0.15) it achieves 100% ASR with perturbation norm ~0.2 (T in {100,1000}, K in {3,5}, d=1000). The authors' proposed defense -- randomly shuffling the first T/2 of the logged data before running the bandit -- significantly (but only partially) reduces attack success; a complete defense is stated as an open problem.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a new threat model for offline multi-armed bandit (MAB) evaluation pipelines: rather than poisoning logged rewards or context samples during training (the standard attack surface in prior bandit-poisoning work), the adversary perturbs the WEIGHTS of the reward model itself, before bandit training begins, while the logged data and training procedure remain untouched. The authors formalize three attack designs against UCB (full-trajectory, trajectory-free, and an online 'Online Score-Aware' (OSA) heuristic), show that for linear reward models r(X)=w^Tx all three reduce to convex quadratic programs (Theorem 3.1), and extend this via a Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) linearization to overparameterized ReLU network reward models (Corollary 3.2). They prove a feasibility guarantee (Theorem 3.3: attacks are feasible with probability 1 once dimension d exceeds (T-K)(K-1) constraints) and a norm bound (Theorem 3.4: the required perturbation's l2-norm shrinks as tilde-O(d^{-1/2}) once d >= KT), i.e. attacks get cheaper as dimensionality grows. They validate on synthetic data and on two real Hugging Face reward models (ImageReward for compositional alignment, the LAION Aesthetic Predictor) used to evaluate five text-to-image generative models (Stable Diffusion 3, Stable Diffusion 1.4, Kandinsky 3, Openjourney, Stable Diffusion XL), and propose a shuffling-based defense.
threat_model: Adversary A has read access to the offline logged datasets D_1,...,D_K (one dataset of i.i.d. samples per arm) but cannot modify the logged samples, and cannot interfere with the bandit's training/selection procedure itself. Its sole point of leverage is to perturb the reward model's parameters before training: w -> w+delta for a linear reward model, or theta -> theta+delta for a neural-network reward model (motivated by the fact that such reward models -- e.g. CLIP/BLIP/VQA-derived scorers, ImageReward, aesthetic predictors -- are frequently distributed with public weights on Hugging Face, so an attacker could realistically supply or tamper with a 'community' checkpoint). The adversary's goal is to keep the perturbation norm ||delta||_2 as small as possible (i.e., stay imperceptible/hard to detect) while forcing the bandit to fail to identify/exploit the true optimal arm i*, either by (a) forcing a fully prescribed arbitrary trajectory of arm pulls (full-trajectory attack), or (b) merely ensuring the optimal arm is never selected at chosen timesteps without dictating exactly which arm is pulled instead (trajectory-free / OSA attacks). This is explicitly contrasted in the paper (Sec 4.8) with prior 'reward-corruption-robust bandit' literature (Lykouris et al. 2018 Fast-Slow, Niss & Tewari 2020 epsilon-contamination), where the adversary is active and present throughout training and directly perturbs realized reward observations; here the adversary acts once, offline, before training starts, and never touches the environment or logged samples.
limitations: The paper explicitly states its own defense only 'partially mitigates' attacks under certain conditions and that 'a complete defense remains open.' The NTK-based linearization for neural-network reward models is only theoretically justified for asymptotically wide networks (formally an O(W_max^{-1}) approximation error), and empirically the attack needs hidden layers with more than 750 neurons to reach 100% ASR, so the guarantees are weaker or unproven for narrow/deep practical reward networks. The threat model assumes the adversary already has read access to the exact logged offline datasets D_1..D_K used for bandit training and can directly write/replace the reward model's weight vector -- a strong assumption realistic mainly in the specific scenario the paper motivates (publicly hosted community reward-model checkpoints on Hugging Face), rather than a general claim about all bandit deployments. The main convexity/QP reduction (Theorem 3.1) and feasibility/norm results (Theorems 3.3-3.4) are proved for the linear reward case, or for the neural case only via linear approximation; the online OSA heuristic's O(log T) constraint growth is described as an empirical observation only ('a full theoretical analysis of the OSA attack is beyond the scope of this paper'). Theorem 3.4's tilde-O(d^{-1/2}) bound requires simplifying assumptions (product measures, Cov(P_i)=I, unit-norm means with near-orthogonality) that the authors state are 'introduced solely to simplify notation' but are formally load-bearing for that specific bound.

datasets_used: Synthetic logged bandit data (varying K, d, T) generated by the authors for Sections 4.1, 4.2, 4.6, 4.7
GenAI-Bench (Li et al., 2024) -- 30 randomly sampled prompts used to generate logged image data
Logged image outputs generated from 5 text-to-image models (Stable Diffusion 3, Stable Diffusion 1.4, Kandinsky 3, Openjourney, Stable Diffusion XL) using random seeds 1 to 100
quantitative_results: All attack variants (OSA, Trajectory-Free, Full-Trajectory) achieve 100% ASR on UCB in synthetic linear/nonlinear experiments (Sec 4.1)
Real-world ImageReward and Aesthetic Predictor attacks: ~80% probability that a randomly sampled prompt yields ASR between 90-100% (Sec 4.3, Fig 3)
Cross-algorithm attack (UCB, ETC, epsilon-greedy): 100% ASR across all three (Table 1, Sec 4.4)
Neural reward-model width sweep: ASR reaches 100% once hidden layer exceeds 750 neurons (Sec 4.7, Fig 4 left)
Random noise control: ASR stays at approximately 25% baseline and remains nearly constant as random perturbation l2-norm increases (Sec 4.6, Fig 4 right)
Attack vs. Fast-Slow robust bandit (Lykouris et al. 2018), corruption parameter 0.5, K in {3,5}, T=1000, d=1000: 100% ASR with perturbation norm ~0.3 (Sec 4.8, Fig 5)
Attack vs. epsilon-contamination UCB (Niss & Tewari 2020), epsilon=0.15, T in {100,1000}, K in {3,5}, d=1000, alpha=0.1, sigma=1: 100% ASR with perturbation norm ~0.2 (Sec 4.8, Fig 6)
Theoretical: feasibility holds w.p. 1 when d > (T-K)(K-1) (Theorem 3.3); attack l2-norm bound O(sqrt(T^3 log T log d / (Kd))) when d >= KT (Theorem 3.4)
Dimensionality sweep (Sec 4.2, Fig 2 left): T=100, K=3, ASR=100% while both l2 and l-infinity perturbation norms decrease as input dimension increases
baselines_compared: Random/naive perturbation of the reward model (shown ineffective, ~25% flat ASR, vs. the paper's targeted optimization)
Fast-Slow robust stochastic bandit algorithm (Lykouris et al., 2018) -- corruption-robust baseline shown to still be vulnerable to the paper's pre-training attack
epsilon-contamination robust UCB (Niss & Tewari, 2020) -- corruption-robust baseline shown to still be vulnerable
Feasibility-Program-derived 'Optimal' QP solution used as an upper-bound reference to measure OSA's optimality gap (Sec 4.2)
UCB, ETC, and epsilon-greedy as the underlying bandit algorithms being attacked (Sec 4.4, Table 1)
